description: First 3D navigable UTE truth topology prototype.

nodes.title: Pythagorean Theorem
nodes.summary: For a right triangle in Euclidean geometry, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides. It is a foundational bridge between geometry, measurement, construction, navigation, and later physics.
nodes.claim: For a right triangle on a flat Euclidean plane: a² + b² = c².

nodes.title: Equality / Identity of Quantity
nodes.summary: Equality is the relation that allows two quantities, symbols, or descriptions to be treated as the same within a defined context. It is a root dependency for arithmetic, logic, measurement, proof, and computation.
nodes.claim: If A = B, then A and B may be substituted for one another in valid contexts without changing truth.

nodes.title: Counting and Natural Numbers
nodes.summary: Counting assigns stable ordered quantities to discrete collections. It allows intelligences to compare, record, communicate, and reason about number.
nodes.claim: Discrete quantities can be represented consistently using ordered symbolic quantities called natural numbers.

nodes.title: Addition
nodes.summary: Addition describes the combination of quantities into a total. It is one of the first scalable operations for arithmetic, resource tracking, engineering, and computation.
nodes.claim: Addition combines quantities into a larger quantity while preserving consistent quantity relationships.

nodes.title: Subtraction
nodes.summary: Subtraction describes removal, comparison, or difference between quantities. It is essential for deficits, measurement gaps, accounting, and change tracking.
nodes.claim: Subtraction describes the removal of a quantity from another quantity, or the difference between two quantities.

nodes.title: Multiplication
nodes.summary: Multiplication compresses repeated addition and enables scaling, area, volume, growth models, engineering calculations, and efficient computation.
nodes.claim: Multiplication represents repeated addition or the scaling of one quantity by another.

nodes.title: Division
nodes.summary: Division partitions quantities into equal parts or determines how many equal groups fit into a quantity. It underlies sharing, rates, fractions, and ratios.
nodes.claim: Division describes partitioning a quantity into equal parts or determining how many equal groups fit within a quantity.

nodes.title: Fractions
nodes.summary: Fractions represent parts of a whole created by equal division. They allow precise reasoning about partial quantities, measurement, probability, and proportional systems.
nodes.claim: Fractions represent quantities formed by dividing a whole into equal parts and selecting some number of those parts.

nodes.title: Ratio
nodes.summary: A ratio compares two quantities. It is essential for scaling, maps, mixtures, mechanics, medicine, and engineering.
nodes.claim: A ratio compares two quantities by expressing how much of one quantity corresponds to another.

nodes.title: Proportion
nodes.summary: Proportion expresses equality between ratios. It supports scale models, similar triangles, map reading, chemical mixtures, and design transfer.
nodes.claim: A proportion states that two ratios are equal within a defined context.

nodes.title: Exponents
nodes.summary: Exponents compress repeated multiplication and describe structured growth, area, volume, scaling, decay, and scientific notation.
nodes.claim: Exponents represent repeated multiplication of a quantity by itself or, more generally, structured scaling relations.

nodes.title: Roots
nodes.summary: Roots invert exponentiation. They are essential for geometry, measurement, inverse scaling, engineering, and physical equations.
nodes.claim: A root is a value which, when multiplied by itself a specified number of times, produces a given quantity.

nodes.title: Negative Numbers
nodes.summary: Negative numbers represent positions or quantities below a defined zero point, including deficits, opposite directions, debts, and signed change.
nodes.claim: Negative numbers represent quantities below a defined zero point, directed opposites, deficits, or inverse positions on an ordered scale.

nodes.title: Approximation
nodes.summary: Approximation represents values close enough for a defined purpose or tolerance. It is critical for measurement, engineering, computation, and scientific uncertainty.
nodes.claim: Approximation represents a value close enough to a target value for a defined purpose, tolerance, or measurement resolution.
